## Usage Examples

### Docker Usage

\```bash
# Initial setup
./docker-build.sh setup

# Edit config files
vim config/proxmox.pkrvars.hcl
vim config/build.pkrvars.hcl
vim config/network.pkrvars.hcl

# Run a build
./docker-build.sh build

# Validate templates
./docker-build.sh validate
\```

### Building Ubuntu 26.04

From the build menu, select option **21 - Ubuntu Server 26.04 LTS**

Or programmatically:
\```bash
docker-compose run --rm packer ./build.sh
# Then select: 21
\```

## Testing

Validate all changes:
\```bash
./docker-build.sh validate
\```

Run Ubuntu QEMU tests:
\```bash
cd tests/ubuntu-qemu
./run.sh
\```

## Migration Notes

### For Existing Users:

1. **No Breaking Changes**: All existing functionality remains unchanged
2. **Optional Docker**: You can continue using native Packer/Ansible installation
3. **Config Files**: No changes needed to existing config files
4. **New Ubuntu 26.04**: Available as menu option 21

### Recommended Migration Path:

1. Pull latest changes
2. Try Docker setup: `./docker-build.sh setup`
3. Copy your existing config files to `./config/`
4. Test with validation: `./docker-build.sh validate`
5. Run your first Docker build

## Next Steps

1. **Documentation**: All Docker usage documented in DOCKER.md
2. **Testing**: Ubuntu 26.04 templates ready for testing
3. **CI/CD**: Docker images can be used in pipelines
4. **Customization**: Both native and Docker workflows supported

## Contributors

- Ubuntu 26.04 support: traefikturkey team
- Docker containerization: Added in this update
- Based on: https://github.com/ajschroeder/proxmox-packer-examples

## Version Info

- Packer: 1.12.0+
- Ansible: 2.14+
- Proxmox: 8.0+
- Docker Image: Ubuntu 24.04 base

## Short Description (for GitHub header):

Infrastructure-as-Code for automated VM template creation on Proxmox using Packer and Ansible. Supports multiple Linux distributions (Ubuntu, Debian, RHEL family, SUSE) and Windows 11. Fully containerized with Docker for portability.

## Longer Description (for README or About section):
```
Automated Proxmox VM Template Builder

Build production-ready virtual machine templates for Proxmox using HashiCorp Packer and Ansible. 
Features include custom storage layouts, static/DHCP networking, cloud-init support, and both 
UEFI/BIOS boot modes. Fully containerized for consistent builds across Windows, macOS, and Linux.

Supported Operating Systems:
• Ubuntu Server (20.04, 22.04, 24.04, 26.04 LTS)
• Debian (11, 12)
• AlmaLinux (8, 9, 10)
• Rocky Linux (8, 9, 10)
• CentOS Stream (9, 10)
• Oracle Linux (8, 9)
• OpenSUSE Leap (15.5, 15.6)
• Windows 11 (Pro, Enterprise)

Quick Start: ./docker-build.sh setup

1	# Oncall - Packer for Proxmox
2	
3	## Table of Contents

18	## Introduction
19	
20	This repository provides opinionated infrastructure-as-code examples to automate the creation of virtual machine images and their guest operating systems on Proxmox using [HashiCorp Packer][packer] and the [Packer Plugin for Proxmox][packer-plugin-proxmox] (`proxmox-iso` builder). All examples are authored in the HashiCorp Configuration Language ("HCL2").
21	
22	By default, the machine image artifacts are converted to templates within Proxmox after a virtual machine is built and configured according to the individual templates.
23	
24	The following builds are available:
25

50	## Requirements
51	
52	**Operating Systems**:
53	
54	Operating systems and versions tested with the project:
55	
56	- Proxmox PVE Version >= 8
57	- Ubuntu Server 22.04 LTS (`x86_64`)
58	- CentOS Stream 9 (`x86_64`)
59	
60	**Packer**:
61	
62	- HashiCorp [Packer][packer-install] 1.12.0 or higher.
63	
64	  > **Note**
65	  >
66	  > Click on the operating system name to display the installation steps.
67	
68	  - <details>
69	      <summary>Ubuntu</summary>
70	
71	    The Terraform packages are signed using a private key controlled by HashiCorp, so you must configure your system to trust that HashiCorp key for package authentication.
72	
73	    To configure your repository:
74	
75	    ```shell
76	    sudo bash -c 'wget -O- https://apt.releases.hashicorp.com/gpg | gpg --dearmor > /usr/share/keyrings/hashicorp-archive-keyring.gpg'
77	    ```
78	
79	    Verify the key's fingerprint:
80	
81	    ```shell
82	    gpg --no-default-keyring --keyring /usr/share/keyrings/hashicorp-archive-keyring.gpg --fingerprint
83	    ```
84	
85	    The fingerprint must match E8A0 32E0 94D8 EB4E A189 D270 DA41 8C88 A321 9F7B. You can also verify the key on [Security at HashiCorp][hcp-security] under Linux Package Checksum Verification.
86	
87	    Add the official HashiCorp repository to your system:
88	
89	    ```shell
90	    sudo bash -c 'echo "deb [signed-by=/usr/share/keyrings/hashicorp-archive-keyring.gpg] \
91	    https://apt.releases.hashicorp.com $(lsb_release -cs) main" > /etc/apt/sources.list.d/hashicorp.list'
92	    ```
93	
94	    Install Packer from HashiCorp repository:
95	
96	    ```shell
97	    sudo apt update && sudo apt install packer
98	    ```
99	
100	    </details>
101	
102	  - <details>
103	      <summary>CentOS Stream 9</summary>
104	
105	    Install `yum-config-manager` to manage your repositories.
106	
107	    ```shell
108	    sudo yum install -y yum-utils
109	    ```
110	
111	    Use `yum-config-manager` to add the official HashiCorp Linux repository:
112	
113	    ```shell
114	    sudo yum-config-manager --add-repo https://rpm.releases.hashicorp.com/RHEL/hashicorp.repo
115	    ```
116	
117	    Install.
118	
119	    ```shell
120	    sudo yum -y install packer
121	    ```
122	    </details>
123	
124	
125	- Packer plugins:
126	
127	  > **Note**
128	  >
129	  > Required plugins are automatically downloaded and initialized when using `./build.sh`. For dark sites, you may download the plugins and place these same directory as your Packer executable `/usr/local/bin` or `$HOME/.packer.d/plugins`.
130	
131	  - [Packer Plugin for Git][packer-plugin-git] 0.6.2 or later - a community plugin for HashiCorp Packer.
132	  - HashiCorp [Packer Plugin for Proxmox-ISO][packer-plugin-proxmox] version 1.2.1 - the plugin for HashiCorp Packer to communicate with Proxmox VE. This needs to be pinned to version 1.2.1 at this time due to a [CPU bug](https://github.com/hashicorp/packer-plugin-proxmox/issues/307).
133	
134	**Ansible**:
135	
136	- [Ansible][ansible] [Core][ansible-core] version 2.14 or higher.
137	
138	  > **Note**
139	  >
140	  > Click on the operating system name to display the installation steps.
141	
142	  - <details>
143	      <summary>Ubuntu</summary>
144	
145	    It is recommended that you install ansible-core using your system's package manager instead of via pip.
146	
147	    Refresh the repositories:
148	    ```shell
149	    sudo apt update
150	    ```
151	
152	    Install software-properties-common:
153	    ```shell
154	    sudo apt install -y software-properties-common
155	    ```
156	
157	    Add the Ansible repository to your system:
158	
159	    ```shell
160	    sudo add-apt-repository --yes --update ppa:ansible/ansible
161	    ```
162	
163	    Install ansible-core from the Ansible repository:
164	
165	    ```shell
166	    sudo apt install -y ansible-core
167	    ```
168	
169	    </details>
170	
171	
172	  - <details>
173	      <summary>CentOS Stream 9</summary>
174	
175	    It is recommended that you install ansible using your system's package manager instead of via pip.
176	
177	    Install.
178	
179	    ```shell
180	    dnf -y install ansible
181	    ```
182	    </details>

184	
185	**Platform**:
186	
187	- Proxmox PVE 8.0 or later.
188	
189	## Docker Setup (Recommended)
190	
191	The easiest way to get started is using Docker, which provides a consistent environment with all dependencies pre-installed.
192	
193	### Prerequisites
194	
195	- [Docker](https://docs.docker.com/get-docker/) installed
196	- [Docker Compose](https://docs.docker.com/compose/install/) installed
197	
198	### Quick Start with Docker
199	
200	1. **Clone the repository:**
201	   ```shell
202	   git clone https://github.com/traefikturkey/oncall.git
203	   cd oncall
204	   ```
205	
206	2. **Create your configuration:**
207	   ```shell
208	   ./config.sh
209	   ```
210	   Edit the files in the `config/` directory with your Proxmox credentials and settings.
211	
212	3. **Build the Docker image:**
213	   ```shell
214	   docker-compose build
215	   ```
216	
217	4. **Run a build:**
218	   ```shell
219	   docker-compose run --rm packer ./build.sh
220	   ```
221	
222	5. **Validate templates:**
223	   ```shell
224	   docker-compose run --rm packer ./validate.sh
225	   ```
226	
227	### Benefits of Docker Setup
228	
229	- **Portability**: Works consistently on Windows, macOS, and Linux
230	- **No dependency installation**: Packer, Ansible, and all tools are pre-configured
231	- **Isolation**: Doesn't affect your host system
232	- **Easy updates**: Rebuild the image to get latest tool versions
233	- **CI/CD ready**: Perfect for automated pipelines
234	
235	For detailed Docker usage including advanced configurations, see [DOCKER.md](DOCKER.md).
236	
237	# Download
238	
239	After installing the required software, the quickest way to get building is to clone this repository.
240	
241	```shell
242	git clone https://github.com/traefikturkey/oncall.git
243	cd oncall
244	```
245	
246	The following table describes the directory structure.
247	
248	| Directory       | Description                                                                              |
249	| :---            | :---                                                                                     |
250	| **`ansible`**   | Contains the Ansible roles to prepare Linux machine image builds.                        |
251	| **`builds`**    | Contains the templates, variables, and configuration files for the machine image builds. |
252	| **`manifests`** | Manifests created after the completion of the machine image builds.                      |
253	
254	
255	# Configuration
256	
257	## Example Variables
258	
259	The project includes example variables files that you can use as a starting point for your own configuration.
260	
261	The [variables][packer-variables] are defined in `.pkrvars.hcl` files.
262	
263	Run the config script `./config.sh` to copy the `.pkrvars.hcl.example` files to a `config` directory.
264	
265	```shell
266	./config.sh
267	./build.sh
268	```
269	
270	The `config` folder is the default folder. You can override the default by passing an alternate value as the first argument.
271	
272	For example:
273	
274	San Francisco: `us-west-1`
275	
276	```shell
277	./config.sh us-west-1
278	./build.sh us-west-1
279	```
280	
281	Los Angeles: `us-west-2`
282	
283	```shell
284	./config.sh us-west-2
285	./build.sh us-west-2
286	```
287	
288	This is useful for the purposes of running machine image builds for different environment.
289	
290	## Configuration Variables
291	
292	## Proxmox Virtual Machine IDs
293	Each template has a preset static vm_id.
294	
295	These can be changed by editing the `./config/*/(linux or windows)-(*).pkr.hcl` files.
296	
297	### Ansible Variables
298	
299	Edit the `config/ansible.pkrvars.hcl` file to configure the credentials for the Ansible account on Linux machine images.
300	
301	```hcl title="config/ansible.pkrvars.hcl"
302	ansible_username = "ansible"
303	ansible_key      = "<public_key>"
304	```
305	
306	**Ansible User Password**
307	
308	A random password is auto-generated for the Ansible user.
309	
310	### Build Variables
311	
312	Edit the `config/build.pkrvars.hcl` file to configure the credentials for the default account on machine images.
313	
314	```hcl title="config/build.pkrvars.hcl"
315	build_username           = "example"
316	build_password           = "<plaintext_password>"
317	build_password_encrypted = "<sha512_encrypted_password>"
318	build_key                = "<public_key>"
319	```
320	
321	You will need to generate a SHA-512 encrypted password for the `build_password_encrypted` using tools like `mkpasswd`.
322	
323	Run the following command to generate a SHA-512 encrypted password:
324	
325	```shell
326	mkpasswd -m sha512crypt
327	```
328	
329	The following output is displayed:
330	
331	```shell
332	Password: ***************
333	[password hash]
334	```
335	
336	Generate a public key for the `build_key` for public key authentication.
337	
338	Run the following command to generate a public key for the `build_key` for public key authentication.
339	
340	```shell
341	ssh-keygen -t ecdsa -b 512 -C "<name@example.com>"
342	```
343	
344	The following output is displayed:
345	
346	```shell
347	Generating public/private ecdsa key pair.
348	Enter file in which to save the key (/Users/example/.ssh/id_ecdsa):
349	Enter passphrase (empty for no passphrase): **************
350	Enter same passphrase again: **************
351	Your identification has been saved in /Users/example/.ssh/id_ecdsa.
352	Your public key has been saved in /Users/example/.ssh/id_ecdsa.pub.
353	```
354	
355	The content of the public key, `build_key`, is added the key to the `~/.ssh/authorized_keys` file of the `build_username` on the Linux guest operating systems.
356	
357	> [!IMPORTANT]
358	>
359	> Make sure to replace the example public keys and passwords!
360	>
361	> By default, both Public Key Authentication and Password Authentication are enabled for Linux distributions.
362	>
363	> If you wish to disable Password Authentication and only use Public Key Authentication, comment or remove the portion of the associated Ansible `configure` role.
364	
365	### Common Variables
366	
367	Edit the `config/common.pkrvars.hcl` file to configure the following common variables:
368	
369	- Removable Media Settings
370	- Boot and Provisioning Settings
371	- HCP Packer Registry
372	
373	```hcl title="config/common.pkrvars.hcl"
374	// Removable Media Settings
375	common_iso_storage = "<Proxmox Storage Location>"
376	
377	// Boot and Provisioning Settings
378	common_data_source       = "http"
379	common_http_interface    = null
380	common_http_bind_address = null
381	common_http_port_min     = 8000
382	common_http_port_max     = 8099
383	common_ip_wait_timeout   = "20m"
384	common_shutdown_timeout  = "15m"
385	
386	// HCP Packer
387	common_hcp_packer_registry_enabled = false
388	```
389	
390	#### Data Source
391	
392	The default provisioning data source for Linux machine image builds is `http`. This is used to serve the kickstart files to the Linux guest operating system during the build.
393	
394	```hcl title="config/common.pkrvars.hcl"
395	common_data_source = "http"
396	```
397	
398	> **Note**
399	>
400	>    Packer includes a built-in HTTP server that is used to serve the kickstart files for Linux machine image builds.
401	>
402	>    If iptables/nftables is enabled on your Packer host, you will need to open `common_http_port_min` through `common_http_port_max` ports.
403	>
404	>    iptables command:
405	>    ```shell
406	>    iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --match multiport --dports 8000:9000 -j ACCEPT
407	>    ```
408	>
409	>    firewall-cmd example:
410	>    ```shell
411	>    firewall-cmd --zone=public --add-port=8000-9000/tcp --permanent
412	>    firewall-cmd --reload
413	>    ```
414	
415	You can change the `common_data_source` from `http` to `disk` to build supported Linux machine images without the need to use Packer's HTTP server. This is useful for environments that may not be able to route back to the system from which Packer is running. For example, building a machine image in VMware Cloud on AWS.
416	
417	```hcl title="config/common.pkrvars.hcl"
418	common_data_source = "disk"
419	```
420	
421	The Packer plugin's `cd_content` option is used when selecting `disk` unless the distribution does not support a secondary CD-ROM.
422	
423	#### HTTP Interface
424	
425	Name of the network interface that Packer gets `HTTPIP` from. Defaults to the first non loopback interface.
426	
427	```hcl title="config/common.pkrvars.hcl"
428	common_http_interface = "eth2"
429	```
430	
431	#### HTTP Bind Address
432	
433	IP address on the build server to bind the Packer HTTP instance to. Must be an interface that is reachable from the Proxmox server.
434	```hcl title="config/common.pkrvars.hcl"
435	common_http_bind_address = 172.16.15.97"
436	```
437	
438	### Network Variables
439	
440	Edit the `config/network.pkrvars.hcl` file to configure the following:
441	
442	#### Proxmox Specific Network Variables for VM Templates
443	These variables are used by Packer to configure the network interface for the VM template. These are specific to your environment. For example, to use the default `vmbr0` interface and the tag for VLAN 102, you would set it as follows:
444	
445	```hcl title="config/network.pkrvars.hcl"
446	// Proxmox settings for VM templates
447	vm_bridge_interface  = "vmbr0"
448	vm_vlan_tag          = "102"
449	```
450	
451	Configuring a static IP address under the `configs/network.pkrvars.hcl` file is supported. If you want to use DHCP for the templates then leave these variables commented out. The default is DHCP.
452	
453	> **Note**
454	>
455	> - These settings are site specific for each Proxmox host and are going to be needed regardless if you use DHCP or static IP addresses.
456	
457	#### Static IP address settings
458	The Packer build templates default to using DHCP, however, you can use static IP addressing for your VM templates. Simply uncomment the following vars and configure to your specific requirements:
459	
460	```hcl title="config/network.pkrvars.hcl"
461	vm_ip_address = "192.168.101.100"
462	vm_ip_netmask = 24
463	vm_ip_gateway = "192.168.101.1"
464	vm_dns_list   = [ "8.8.8.8", "8.8.4.4" ]
465	```
466	
467	> **Note**
468	>
469	> - If you need/want to go back to using DHCP, just comment these variables out again and the templates should go back to using DHCP.
470	
471	### Proxmox Variables
472	
473	Edit the `config/proxmox.pkrvars.hcl` file to configure the following:
474	
475	- Promxox Endpoint and Credentials
476	
477	```hcl title="config/proxmox.pkrvars.hcl"
478	// Proxmox Credentials
479	proxmox_api_token_id        = "name@realm!token"
480	proxmox_api_token_secret    = "<token secret>"
481	proxmox_insecure_connection = false
482	
483	// Proxmox Specific Settings
484	proxmox_hostname = "<FQDN or IP of proxmox server>"
485	proxmox_node     = "<proxmox node name>"
486	```
487	
488	The `proxmox_api_token_id` variable uses a specific format and, as the time of this writing, needs to be assigned to the `PVEAdmin` role. One of the to-do's is to document a least-privilege method of creating the Proxmox API token.
489	
490	For more information, please see the [Proxmox documentation][proxmox-api-tokens] on authentication.
491	
492	For Proxmox installs that use a self-signed certificate, you will want to set `proxmox_insecure_connection` to `true`.
493	
494	### Storage Variables
495	
496	Edit the `config/linux-storage.pkrvars.hcl` file to configure storage for VM templates.
497	
498	#### Disk Device
499	
500	```hcl
501	// VM Storage Settings
502	vm_disk_device     = "vda"
503	```
504	
505	`vm_disk_device`:`string` - This variable depends on the disk controller used inside of the specific `.auto.pkrvars.hcl` file. By default, the builds use the `virtio-scsi-pci` disk controller and that requires the use of `vda`. If you decide to use a non-virtio controller, then you'll have to change the `vm_disk_device` variable to the appropriate device.
506	
507	#### EFI Device
508	```hcl
509	vm_efi_storage_pool      = "pool0"
510	vm_efi_type              = "4m"
511	vm_efi_pre_enrolled_keys = false
512	```
513	
514	#### Disk Partitions
515	
516	`vm_disk_partitions`:`list[dict]` - Use this list to define the primary partitions that will be created when a specific build runs. Each of the builds process this list in order, so the first partition defined in the list will be the first partition created, the second one listed will be the second one created, and so on.
517	
518	> **Note**
519	>
520	> - All partition sizes are in MegaBytes (MB)
521	> - If you want to have a partition consume all available free space, you can indicate that with `-1`
522	
523	##### Partitioning Examples
524	
525	<details>
526	  <summary>Single Partition Example for BIOS bootloaders</summary>
527	Below is an example of a partition layout for a VM template that boots with BIOS and uses a single partition for the OS.
528	
529	```hcl title="config/linux-storage.pkrvars.hcl"
530	// VM Storage Settings
531	vm_disk_device     = "vda"
532	vm_disk_use_swap   = true
533	vm_disk_partitions = [
534	  {
535	    name = "root"
536	    size = -1,
537	    format = {
538	      label  = "ROOTFS",
539	      fstype = "ext4",
540	    },
541	    mount = {
542	      path    = "/",
543	      options = "",
544	    },
545	    volume_group = "",
546	  },
547	]
548	```
549	</details>
550	
551	<details>
552	  <summary>Single Partition Example for UEFI bootloaders</summary>
553	This example is similar to the above example except that it has the extra partitions needed for the UEFI (OVMF) bootloader. Note the extra variables for the EFI settings.
554	
555	```hcl title="config/linux-storage.pkrvars.hcl"
556	// VM EFI Settings
557	vm_efi_storage_pool      = "pool0"
558	vm_efi_type              = "4m"
559	vm_efi_pre_enrolled_keys = false
560	
561	// VM Storage Settings
562	vm_disk_device     = "vda"
563	vm_disk_use_swap   = true
564	vm_disk_partitions = [
565	  {
566	    name = "efi"
567	    size = 1024,
568	    format = {
569	      label  = "EFIFS",
570	      fstype = "fat32",
571	    },
572	    mount = {
573	      path    = "/boot/efi",
574	      options = "",
575	    },
576	    volume_group = "",
577	  },
578	  {
579	    name = "boot"
580	    size = 1024,
581	    format = {
582	      label  = "BOOTFS",
583	      fstype = "ext4",
584	    },
585	    mount = {
586	      path    = "/boot",
587	      options = "",
588	    },
589	    volume_group = "",
590	  },
591	  {
592	    name = "root"
593	    size = -1,
594	    format = {
595	      label  = "ROOTFS",
596	      fstype = "ext4",
597	    },
598	    mount = {
599	      path    = "/",
600	      options = "",
601	    },
602	    volume_group = "",
603	  },
604	]
605	```
606	</details>
607	
608	<details>
609	  <summary>LVM Partitioning Example with CIS partitions for UEFI bootloaders</summary>
610	This is a more complex example of a partition layout for a VM template that uses LVM and has volumes with mount options required by CIS for hardening a linux system.
611	
612	
613	```hcl title="config/linux-storage.pkrvars.hcl"
614	//VM EFI Settings
615	vm_efi_storage_pool      = "pool0"
616	vm_efi_type              = "4m"
617	vm_efi_pre_enrolled_keys = false
618	
619	// UEFI VM Storage Settings
620	vm_disk_device     = "vda"
621	vm_disk_use_swap   = true
622	vm_disk_partitions = [
623	  {
624	    name = "efi"
625	    size = 1024,
626	    format = {
627	      label  = "EFIFS",
628	      fstype = "fat32",
629	    },
630	    mount = {
631	      path    = "/boot/efi",
632	      options = "",
633	    },
634	    volume_group = "",
635	  },
636	  {
637	    name = "boot"
638	    size = 1024,
639	    format = {
640	      label  = "BOOTFS",
641	      fstype = "ext4",
642	    },
643	    mount = {
644	      path    = "/boot",
645	      options = "",
646	    },
647	    volume_group = "",
648	  },
649	  {
650	    name = "sysvg"
651	    size = -1,
652	    format = {
653	      label  = "",
654	      fstype = "",
655	    },
656	    mount = {
657	      path    = "",
658	      options = "",
659	    },
660	    volume_group = "sysvg",
661	  },
662	]
663	vm_disk_lvm = [
664	  {
665	    name: "sysvg",
666	    partitions: [
667	      {
668	        name = "lv_swap",
669	        size = 1024,
670	        format = {
671	          label  = "SWAPFS",
672	          fstype = "swap",
673	        },
674	        mount = {
675	          path    = "",
676	          options = "",
677	        },
678	      },
679	      {
680	        name = "lv_root",
681	        size = 10240,
682	        format = {
683	          label  = "ROOTFS",
684	          fstype = "ext4",
685	        },
686	        mount = {
687	          path    = "/",
688	          options = "",
689	        },
690	      },
691	      {
692	        name = "lv_home",
693	        size = 4096,
694	        format = {
695	          label  = "HOMEFS",
696	          fstype = "ext4",
697	        },
698	        mount = {
699	          path    = "/home",
700	          options = "nodev,nosuid",
701	        },
702	      },
703	      {
704	        name = "lv_opt",
705	        size = 2048,
706	        format = {
707	          label  = "OPTFS",
708	          fstype = "ext4",
709	        },
710	        mount = {
711	          path    = "/opt",
712	          options = "nodev",
713	        },
714	      },
715	      {
716	        name = "lv_tmp",
717	        size = 4096,
718	        format = {
719	          label  = "TMPFS",
720	          fstype = "ext4",
721	        },
722	        mount = {
723	          path    = "/tmp",
724	          options = "nodev,noexec,nosuid",
725	        },
726	      },
727	      {
728	        name = "lv_var",
729	        size = 2048,
730	        format = {
731	          label  = "VARFS",
732	          fstype = "ext4",
733	        },
734	        mount = {
735	          path    = "/var",
736	          options = "nodev",
737	        },
738	      },
739	      {
740	        name = "lv_var_tmp",
741	        size = 1000,
742	        format = {
743	          label  = "VARTMPFS",
744	          fstype = "ext4",
745	        },
746	        mount = {
747	          path    = "/var/tmp",
748	          options = "nodev,noexec,nosuid",
749	        },
750	      },
751	      {
752	        name = "lv_var_log",
753	        size = 4096,
754	        format = {
755	          label  = "VARLOGFS",
756	          fstype = "ext4",
757	        },
758	        mount = {
759	          path    = "/var/log",
760	          options = "nodev,noexec,nosuid",
761	        },
762	      },
763	      {
764	        name = "lv_var_audit",
765	        size = 500,
766	        format = {
767	          label  = "AUDITFS",
768	          fstype = "ext4",
769	        },
770	        mount = {
771	          path    = "/var/log/audit",
772	          options = "nodev,noexec,nosuid",

774	      },

776	  }

779	</details>
780	
781	
782	## Packer Machine Image Builds
783	
784	Edit the `<build type>.pkrvars.hcl` file in the `config` directory for each type of build to set the following virtual machine hardware settings, as required:
785	
786	- CPUs `(int)`
787	- CPU Cores `(int)`
788	- Memory in MB `(int)`
789	- Primary Disk in MB `(string)` (e.g. 32GB)
790	- .iso Path `(string)`
791	- .iso File `(string)`
792	
793	```hcl title="config/linux-ubuntu-22-04-lts.pkrvars.hcl"
794	// Guest Operating System Metadata
795	vm_os_language   = "en_US"
796	vm_os_keyboard   = "us"
797	vm_os_timezone   = "UTC"
798	vm_os_family     = "linux"
799	vm_os_name       = "ubuntu"
800	vm_os_version    = "22.04-lts"
801	
802	// Virtual Machine Guest Operating System Setting
803	vm_os_type       = "l26"
804	vm_cloudinit     = true
805	
806	// Virtual Machine Hardware Settings
807	vm_bios                 = "ovmf"
808	vm_cpu_count            = 1
809	vm_cpu_sockets          = 1
810	vm_cpu_type             = "kvm64"
811	vm_mem_size             = 2048
812	vm_disk_type            = "virtio"
813	vm_disk_size            = "32G"
814	vm_disk_format          = "raw"
815	vm_disk_controller_type = "virtio-scsi-pci"
816	vm_network_card_model   = "virtio"
817	
818	// Removable Media Settings
819	iso_path     = "iso"
820	iso_file     = "ubuntu-22.04-live-server-amd64.iso"
821	// The checksum can be a URL or an actual checksum value. URL is preferred
822	iso_checksum = "file:https://releases.ubuntu.com/jammy/SHA256SUMS"
823	
824	// Boot Settings
825	vm_boot      = "order=virtio0;ide2;net0"
826	vm_boot_wait = "5s"
827	
828	// EFI Settings
829	vm_firmware_path         = "./OVMF.fd"
830	```
831	
832	> **Note**
833	>
834	>   All `config/<build type>.pkrvars.hcl` default to using:
835	>   - VirtIO SCSI storage device
836	>   - VirtIO (paravirtualized) network card device
837	>   - UEFI boot firmware
838	
839	The defaults use VirtIO to balance out performance, compatibility, and ease of use. Feel free to change the storage and network controllers to suit your needs. However, if you change the storage or network controllers and run into issues you should change them back to defaults and try the builds again. I won't support any builds that don't use the VirtIO drivers.
840	
841	Both UEFI and BIOS booting are supported for builds. Inside the `<build type>.pkrvars.hcl` file specific to the build, you can set the `vm_bios` variable to either `seabios` for BIOS or `ovmf` for UEFI booting.
842	
843	> **Note**
844	>
845	>   The storage layouts are different for each bootloader type so you'll need to configure the storage layouts accordingly.
846	
847	# Terraform
848	An example Terraform for a UEFI Ubuntu 24.04 template is included.  For more information [go here](example_uefi_ubuntu_terraform/README.md)
849	
850	There is also a script to import an existing vm to use as an example for building Terraform builds.  For more information [go here](terraform_import/README.md)
851	
852	### Cloud-Init
853	All builds for operating systems that support [cloud-init][cloud-init] now have the option to enable it. This can be done on a per-build basis inside the `<build type>.pkrvars.hcl` files in the `config` directory. The default setting is `true`.
854	
855	If a particular linux distribution ships with cloud-init (e.g. Ubuntu) and cloud-init is set to `false` in the `config/<build type>.pkrvars.hcl` file for the build, then cloud-init will be disabled in the operating system **and** within Proxmox for that specific template.
856	
857	You can also change the Cloud-Init storage backend using the `vm_cloudinit_disk_type` variable. See the [Packer Proxmox Integration docs](https://developer.hashicorp.com/packer/integrations/hashicorp/proxmox/latest/components/builder/iso) for more details.
858	
859	# Known Issues
860	
861	## Windows Builds
862	For obvious reasons, product keys and actual ISO names are not provided for any of the Windows builds in this repository. For example, if you wanted to build a Windows 11 template VM, the Windows 11 build uses the Windows 11 Enterprise Eval ISO. By default packer will build the Windows 11 Enterprise Eval whether you choose the Pro or Enterprise build when running `build.sh`. Also, this build by default should not require any interventions by a human.
863	
864	If you have a valid product key and want to build a Windows 11 Pro or Enterprise template VM, you will need to change the following variables (if building Pro you don't need to change Enterprise vars and vice versa):
865	
866	```hcl title="config/windows-desktop-11.pkrvars.hcl"
867	vm_inst_os_image_pro = "Windows 11 Enterprise Evaluation"   <-- If building Professional with a valid key, you will need to enter in whatever the name of the Operating System is when the Windows install prompts you
868	vm_inst_os_key_pro   = "XXXXX-XXXXX-XXXXX-XXXXX-XXXXX"
869	vm_inst_os_image_ent = "Windows 11 Enterprise Evaluation"   <-- If building Enterprise with a valid key, you can remove the Evaluation from this string
870	vm_inst_os_key_ent   = "XXXXX-XXXXX-XXXXX-XXXXX-XXXXX"
871	...
872	iso_file             = "Name-of-Windows-11-Non-Evaluation-DVD.iso"  <-- This is the name of the real Windows 11 DVD
873	```
874	
875	If your template build hangs or times out, re-run it and then open the console of the template VM and see if Windows is waiting for you to select an Operating System. Select the Operating System version and then click Next and the build should work hands-off the rest of the way. If you want this to be completely hands-off, just change the `vm_inst_os_image_pro` and/or `vm_inst_os_image_ent` vars to match the Operating System selection entry exactly. Then the next time you execute the particular Windows template build, it shouldn't get stuck waiting for input.
876

# MTEA FSG Infrastructure Automation

**Modoc Tribal Enterprise Authority (MTEA)**  
**Federal Services Group (FSG)**  
Network Automation & Infrastructure-as-Code

## Table of Contents

1. [Introduction](#introduction)
1. [Requirements](#requirements)
1. [Docker Setup (Recommended)](#docker-setup-recommended)
1. [Getting Started](#getting-started)
1. [Configuration](#configuration)
1. [Building VM Templates](#building-vm-templates)
1. [Terraform Integration](#terraform-integration)
1. [Troubleshooting](#troubleshooting)

1. [Contributing](#contributing)
1. [Credits](#credits)

## Introduction

This repository provides MTEA FSG's infrastructure-as-code tooling for automating the creation of virtual machine templates on Proxmox. Built with [HashiCorp Packer][packer] and [Ansible][ansible], these tools enable consistent, repeatable deployment of standardized VM images across the MTEA FSG network infrastructure.

All templates are authored in HashiCorp Configuration Language (HCL2) and follow infrastructure-as-code best practices for version control, reproducibility, and documentation.

### Features

- **Automated VM Template Creation**: Build production-ready templates with a single command
- **Multi-Distribution Support**: Linux and Windows operating systems
- **Customizable Storage Layouts**: Support for simple partitions, LVM, and CIS-compliant configurations
- **Network Flexibility**: Static IP or DHCP configuration
- **Cloud-Init Ready**: Modern cloud-init support for rapid provisioning
- **Dual Boot Support**: UEFI and BIOS bootloader options
- **Containerized Workflow**: Docker-based tooling for consistency across platforms

## Requirements

**Infrastructure Platform**:
- Proxmox VE 8.0 or later

**Development Environment** (if not using Docker):
- Ubuntu Server 22.04 LTS or CentOS Stream 9
- HashiCorp [Packer][packer-install] 1.12.0 or higher
- [Ansible Core][ansible-core] 2.14 or higher

**Packer Plugins** (auto-downloaded):
- [Packer Plugin for Git][packer-plugin-git] 0.6.2+
- [Packer Plugin for Proxmox][packer-plugin-proxmox] 1.2.1 (pinned due to [CPU bug](https://github.com/hashicorp/packer-plugin-proxmox/issues/307))

> **Note**: For manual installation instructions, expand the sections below. We recommend using the [Docker setup](#docker-setup-recommended) instead.

<details>
  <summary>Ubuntu - Manual Packer Installation</summary>

\```shell
sudo bash -c 'wget -O- https://apt.releases.hashicorp.com/gpg | gpg --dearmor > /usr/share/keyrings/hashicorp-archive-keyring.gpg'
sudo bash -c 'echo "deb [signed-by=/usr/share/keyrings/hashicorp-archive-keyring.gpg] \
https://apt.releases.hashicorp.com $(lsb_release -cs) main" > /etc/apt/sources.list.d/hashicorp.list'
sudo apt update && sudo apt install packer
\```
</details>

<details>
  <summary>CentOS/RHEL - Manual Packer Installation</summary>

\```shell
sudo yum install -y yum-utils
sudo yum-config-manager --add-repo https://rpm.releases.hashicorp.com/RHEL/hashicorp.repo
sudo yum -y install packer
\```
</details>

<details>
  <summary>Ubuntu - Manual Ansible Installation</summary>

\```shell
sudo apt update
sudo apt install -y software-properties-common
sudo add-apt-repository --yes --update ppa:ansible/ansible
sudo apt install -y ansible-core
\```
</details>

<details>
  <summary>CentOS/RHEL - Manual Ansible Installation</summary>

\```shell
sudo dnf -y install ansible
\```
</details>

## Docker Setup (Recommended)

The easiest and most consistent way to use this tooling is via Docker. This provides:

- **Portability**: Works identically on Windows, macOS, and Linux
- **No Manual Dependencies**: All tools pre-installed and configured
- **Isolation**: Clean separation from host system
- **Version Consistency**: Entire team uses identical tool versions
- **CI/CD Ready**: Perfect for automated pipelines

### Prerequisites

- [Docker](https://docs.docker.com/get-docker/) installed
- [Docker Compose](https://docs.docker.com/compose/install/) installed
- Access to MTEA FSG Proxmox infrastructure

### Quick Start with Docker

1. **Clone the repository:**
   \```shell
   git clone <repository-url>
   cd <repository-name>
   \```

2. **Initial setup:**
   \```shell
   ./docker-build.sh setup
   \```
   This builds the Docker image and creates configuration templates.

3. **Configure for MTEA FSG infrastructure:**
   
   Edit the files in `config/` directory with MTEA FSG Proxmox settings:
   - `proxmox.pkrvars.hcl` - Proxmox API credentials and node information
   - `build.pkrvars.hcl` - VM build user credentials
   - `ansible.pkrvars.hcl` - Ansible automation user credentials
   - `network.pkrvars.hcl` - MTEA FSG network configuration (VLANs, subnets)
   - `linux-storage.pkrvars.hcl` - Storage pool and partition layouts

4. **Build a VM template:**
   \```shell
   ./docker-build.sh build
   \```
   Select the desired OS from the interactive menu.

5. **Validate all templates:**
   \```shell
   ./docker-build.sh validate
   \```

### Docker Helper Commands

\```shell
./docker-build.sh setup      # Initial setup (first time only)
./docker-build.sh build      # Run interactive build menu
./docker-build.sh validate   # Validate all Packer templates
./docker-build.sh shell      # Open shell in container for debugging
./docker-build.sh clean      # Remove Docker images and containers
./docker-build.sh rebuild    # Rebuild Docker image from scratch
\```

For detailed Docker usage, advanced configurations, and troubleshooting, see [DOCKER.md](DOCKER.md).

For a comprehensive setup guide, see [SETUP.md](SETUP.md).

## Getting Started

### Directory Structure

| Directory       | Description                                                                |
| :---            | :---                                                                       |
| **`ansible`**   | Ansible roles for configuring Linux and Windows templates                 |
| **`builds`**    | Packer templates, variables, and build configurations per OS              |
| **`config`**    | Your MTEA FSG-specific configuration files (created by `./config.sh`)    |
| **`manifests`** | Build manifests and metadata (auto-generated after successful builds)     |
| **`tests`**     | Test suites for validating template functionality                         |

### Configuration Script

The `config.sh` script copies example configuration files to create your environment-specific settings:

\```shell
./config.sh
\```

This creates a `config/` directory with templates you'll customize for MTEA FSG infrastructure.

#### Multiple Environment Support

You can maintain separate configurations for different MTEA FSG environments:

\```shell
# Development environment
./config.sh dev
./build.sh dev

# Production environment  
./config.sh prod
./build.sh prod
\```

## Configuration

### Proxmox Connection

Edit `config/proxmox.pkrvars.hcl`:

\```hcl
// Proxmox API Credentials
proxmox_api_token_id        = "packer@pam!packer-token"
proxmox_api_token_secret    = "<api-token-secret>"
proxmox_insecure_connection = true  // Set to false with valid TLS cert

// Proxmox Infrastructure
proxmox_hostname = "proxmox.mtea.local"
proxmox_node     = "pve-node-01"
\```

**API Token Setup**: The API token must have appropriate Proxmox permissions. Currently requires `PVEAdmin` role. See [Proxmox API documentation][proxmox-api-tokens] for token creation.

### Build Credentials

Edit `config/build.pkrvars.hcl`:

\```hcl
build_username           = "mtea-admin"
build_password           = "<plaintext-password>"
build_password_encrypted = "<sha512-hash>"
build_key                = "<ssh-public-key>"
\```

Generate SHA-512 password hash:
\```shell
mkpasswd -m sha512crypt
\```

Generate SSH key pair:
\```shell
ssh-keygen -t ecdsa -b 521 -C "mtea-fsg-automation"
\```

### Ansible Automation User

Edit `config/ansible.pkrvars.hcl`:

\```hcl
ansible_username = "ansible"
ansible_key      = "<ansible-ssh-public-key>"
\```

A random password is auto-generated for the Ansible user. The SSH public key enables passwordless automation.

### Network Configuration

Edit `config/network.pkrvars.hcl` with MTEA FSG network settings:

\```hcl
// Proxmox Network Settings
vm_bridge_interface = "vmbr0"
vm_vlan_tag         = "100"  // MTEA FSG VLAN

// Static IP Configuration (optional - defaults to DHCP)
// vm_ip_address = "10.10.10.100"
// vm_ip_netmask = 24
// vm_ip_gateway = "10.10.10.1"
// vm_dns_list   = ["10.10.10.10", "10.10.10.11"]
\```

**DHCP vs Static IP**: By default, templates use DHCP. Uncomment the static IP variables to configure a fixed address during template build. In production, you'll typically use cloud-init or Terraform to set final IP addresses when cloning templates.

### Storage Configuration

Edit `config/linux-storage.pkrvars.hcl`:

\```hcl
// Storage Pool
vm_storage_pool     = "local-lvm"
vm_efi_storage_pool = "local-lvm"

// Disk Settings
vm_disk_device     = "vda"  // Use with virtio-scsi
vm_disk_use_swap   = true

// Partition Layout
vm_disk_partitions = [
  // See examples below
]
\```

**Partition Layout Options**:

<details>
  <summary>Simple Single Partition (UEFI)</summary>

\```hcl
vm_disk_partitions = [
  {
    name = "efi"
    size = 1024,
    format = { label = "EFIFS", fstype = "fat32" },
    mount = { path = "/boot/efi", options = "" },
    volume_group = "",
  },
  {
    name = "boot"
    size = 1024,
    format = { label = "BOOTFS", fstype = "ext4" },
    mount = { path = "/boot", options = "" },
    volume_group = "",
  },
  {
    name = "root"
    size = -1,  // Use all remaining space
    format = { label = "ROOTFS", fstype = "ext4" },
    mount = { path = "/", options = "" },
    volume_group = "",
  },
]
\```
</details>

<details>
  <summary>LVM with CIS-Compliant Hardening</summary>

For security-hardened systems following CIS benchmarks:

\```hcl
vm_disk_partitions = [
  { name = "efi", size = 1024, format = { label = "EFIFS", fstype = "fat32" },
    mount = { path = "/boot/efi", options = "" }, volume_group = "" },
  { name = "boot", size = 1024, format = { label = "BOOTFS", fstype = "ext4" },
    mount = { path = "/boot", options = "" }, volume_group = "" },
  { name = "sysvg", size = -1, format = { label = "", fstype = "" },
    mount = { path = "", options = "" }, volume_group = "sysvg" },
]

vm_disk_lvm = [
  {
    name: "sysvg",
    partitions: [
      { name = "lv_root", size = 10240, format = { label = "ROOTFS", fstype = "ext4" },
        mount = { path = "/", options = "" }},
      { name = "lv_home", size = 4096, format = { label = "HOMEFS", fstype = "ext4" },
        mount = { path = "/home", options = "nodev,nosuid" }},
      { name = "lv_tmp", size = 4096, format = { label = "TMPFS", fstype = "ext4" },
        mount = { path = "/tmp", options = "nodev,noexec,nosuid" }},
      { name = "lv_var", size = 2048, format = { label = "VARFS", fstype = "ext4" },
        mount = { path = "/var", options = "nodev" }},
      { name = "lv_var_log", size = 4096, format = { label = "VARLOGFS", fstype = "ext4" },
        mount = { path = "/var/log", options = "nodev,noexec,nosuid" }},
      { name = "lv_var_audit", size = 500, format = { label = "AUDITFS", fstype = "ext4" },
        mount = { path = "/var/log/audit", options = "nodev,noexec,nosuid" }},
    ],
  }
]
\```
</details>

### Common Build Settings

Edit `config/common.pkrvars.hcl`:

\```hcl
// ISO Storage Location
common_iso_storage = "local"  // Proxmox storage containing ISO files

// HTTP Server for Kickstart Files
common_data_source       = "http"
common_http_port_min     = 8000
common_http_port_max     = 8099
common_ip_wait_timeout   = "20m"
common_shutdown_timeout  = "15m"
\```

**Data Source Options**:
- `http`: Packer's built-in HTTP server (requires network routing from Proxmox to build host)
- `disk`: Mount kickstart files via secondary CD-ROM (use in isolated networks)

**Firewall Configuration**: If using `http` data source, ensure ports 8000-8099 are open:

\```shell
# iptables
iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --match multiport --dports 8000:8099 -j ACCEPT

# firewalld
firewall-cmd --zone=public --add-port=8000-8099/tcp --permanent
firewall-cmd --reload
\```

### Per-OS Template Settings

Each OS has a configuration file in `config/` directory:

Example `config/linux-ubuntu-24-04-lts.pkrvars.hcl`:

\```hcl
// Guest OS Metadata
vm_os_language = "en_US"
vm_os_keyboard = "us"
vm_os_timezone = "America/Los_Angeles"  // MTEA FSG timezone

// Hardware Configuration
vm_cpu_count    = 2
vm_cpu_sockets  = 1
vm_cpu_type     = "host"  // Use "host" for best performance
vm_mem_size     = 4096
vm_disk_size    = "40G"
vm_disk_type    = "virtio"
vm_disk_format  = "raw"

// Boot Configuration
vm_bios         = "ovmf"  // UEFI (or "seabios" for BIOS)
vm_cloudinit    = true

// ISO Location
iso_path     = "iso"
iso_file     = "ubuntu-24.04-live-server-amd64.iso"
iso_checksum = "https://releases.ubuntu.com/noble/SHA256SUMS"
\```

## Building VM Templates

### Interactive Build Menu

\```shell
./build.sh
\```

This displays an interactive menu where you can select the OS template to build.

### Build Process

1. Packer downloads required plugins (first run only)
2. Creates a temporary VM in Proxmox
3. Boots from ISO and performs automated installation
4. Runs Ansible provisioning (updates, packages, hardening)
5. Converts VM to a reusable template
6. Generates build manifest in `manifests/`

### Template Features

All templates include:
- **QEMU Guest Agent**: For improved VM management and monitoring
- **SSH Access**: Configured with provided keys
- **Ansible User**: Ready for configuration management
- **Cloud-Init** (where supported): For rapid customization on clone
- **Current Updates**: Latest packages at build time

### Debug Mode

Run builds in debug mode for troubleshooting:

\```shell
./build.sh --debug
\```

This enables:
- Verbose Packer output
- Pause on errors with interactive prompt
- Detailed logging

### Validation

Validate all Packer templates without building:

\```shell
./validate.sh
\```

This checks:
- Template syntax
- Variable definitions
- Plugin compatibility

## Terraform Integration

Use Terraform to deploy VMs from your templates. Example configurations included:

- `example_uefi_ubuntu_terraform/` - Complete Ubuntu deployment example
- `terraform_import/` - Import existing VMs as Terraform resources

See the respective README files for detailed Terraform usage.

## Cloud-Init Support

All modern Linux templates support cloud-init for provisioning customization:

\```hcl
// In per-OS config file
vm_cloudinit = true
vm_cloudinit_disk_type = "ide"  // or "scsi", "virtio"
\```

When cloning templates, use cloud-init to:
- Set hostname and network configuration
- Inject SSH keys
- Run custom scripts on first boot
- Configure users and packages

Disable cloud-init by setting `vm_cloudinit = false` in the OS-specific config file.

## Troubleshooting

### Build Timeouts

Increase timeout in OS-specific config:

\```hcl
// In variables.pkr.hcl
variable "timeout" {
  default = "90m"  // Increase if needed
}
\```

### ISO Not Found

1. Verify ISO uploaded to Proxmox storage
2. Check `common_iso_storage` matches Proxmox storage name
3. Confirm `iso_path` and `iso_file` are correct

### Network Connectivity Issues

1. Verify `vm_bridge_interface` exists in Proxmox
2. Check VLAN tag matches your network configuration  
3. Ensure firewall allows Packer HTTP ports (8000-8099)
4. Test connectivity: `curl http://<packer-host>:8000/`

### SSH Connection Failures

1. Verify build credentials in `config/build.pkrvars.hcl`
2. Check Proxmox firewall rules
3. Ensure VM has network connectivity
4. Verify QEMU guest agent is starting

### Packer Plugin Errors

Re-initialize plugins:

\```shell
cd builds/linux/ubuntu/24-04-lts/
packer init .
\```

## Known Issues

### Windows Builds

Windows builds require:
- Valid product keys (not included)
- Correct ISO naming in configuration
- VirtIO drivers for storage and network

See `config/windows-desktop-11.pkrvars.hcl.example` for details.

### Proxmox API Permissions

Currently requires `PVEAdmin` role. Least-privilege configuration documentation is in progress.

### Packer Proxmox Plugin

Pinned to version 1.2.1 due to [CPU configuration bug](https://github.com/hashicorp/packer-plugin-proxmox/issues/307) in newer versions.

## Contributing

MTEA FSG team members: contributions are welcome! Please:

1. Create a feature branch from `main`
2. Test your changes with `./validate.sh`
3. Document any new configurations
4. Submit a pull request with clear description

For bugs or feature requests, open an issue in the repository.

```text
1	# Docker Usage Guide
2	
3	This guide explains how to use the containerized version of MTEA FSG Infrastructure Automation.
4	
5	## Quick Start
6	
7	### Build the Docker Image
8	
9	```bash
10	docker-compose build
11	```
12	
13	### Run Interactively
14	
15	```bash
16	docker-compose run --rm packer
17	```
18	
19	This will drop you into a bash shell inside the container with all tools installed.
20	
21	### Run a Specific Build
22	
23	From inside the container:
24	
25	```bash
26	./build.sh
27	```
28	
29	Then select your desired build from the menu.
30	
31	### Run with Debug Mode
32	
33	```bash
34	docker-compose run --rm packer ./build.sh --debug
35	```
36	
37	## Configuration
38	
39	### Mounting Config Directory
40	
41	The `config` directory is automatically mounted as a volume. This allows you to:
42	
43	1. **Create your configuration on the host:**
44	   ```bash
45	   ./config.sh
46	   ```
47	
48	2. **Edit configuration files on your host machine** using your favorite editor
49	
50	3. **Run builds in the container** with your host configuration:
51	   ```bash
52	   docker-compose run --rm packer ./build.sh
53	   ```
54	
55	### Persisting Build Artifacts
56	
57	The `manifests` directory is also mounted as a volume, so all build manifests will persist on your host machine even after the container is removed.
58	
59	## Advanced Usage
60	
61	### Custom Config Path
62	
63	To use a different config directory:
64	
65	```bash
66	docker-compose run --rm packer ./build.sh /workspace/my-custom-config
67	```
68	
69	### SSH Keys
70	
71	If your builds require SSH keys for authentication, they're automatically mounted read-only from `~/.ssh` on your host.
72	
73	### Environment Variables
74	
75	Set Packer logging:
76	
77	```bash
78	PACKER_LOG=1 docker-compose run --rm packer ./build.sh
79	```
80	
81	Or add to `docker-compose.yml`:
82	
83	```yaml
84	environment:
85	  - PACKER_LOG=1
86	  - PACKER_LOG_PATH=/workspace/packer.log
87	```
88	
89	### Validation
90	
91	Validate all Packer templates:
92	
93	```bash
94	docker-compose run --rm packer ./validate.sh
95	```
96	
97	### Network Mode
98	
99	The container uses `host` network mode by default to ensure Packer can communicate with your Proxmox server and serve HTTP content for kickstart files. If you need different networking, modify the `network_mode` in `docker-compose.yml`.
100	
101	## Building Without Docker Compose
102	
103	If you prefer not to use docker-compose:
104	
105	### Build the image:
106	
107	```bash
108	docker build -t mtea-fsg-automation:latest .
109	```
110	
111	### Run interactively:
112	
113	```bash
114	docker run -it --rm \
115	  --network host \
116	  -v "$(pwd):/workspace" \
117	  -v "$(pwd)/config:/workspace/config" \
118	  -v "$(pwd)/manifests:/workspace/manifests" \
119	  -v "$HOME/.ssh:/root/.ssh:ro" \
120	  mtea-fsg-automation:latest
121	```
122	
123	### Run a single command:
124	
125	```bash
126	docker run -it --rm \
127	  --network host \
128	  -v "$(pwd):/workspace" \
129	  -v "$(pwd)/config:/workspace/config" \
130	  mtea-fsg-automation:latest \
131	  ./validate.sh
132	```
133	
134	## Troubleshooting
135	
136	### Permission Issues
137	
138	If you encounter permission issues with mounted volumes, you may need to adjust ownership:
139	
140	```bash
141	sudo chown -R $(id -u):$(id -g) config/ manifests/
142	```
143	
144	### Proxmox Connection Issues
145	
146	Ensure:
147	1. The container can reach your Proxmox server (check `proxmox_hostname` in config)
148	2. Network mode is set to `host` in docker-compose.yml
149	3. Your firewall allows the HTTP server ports (default 8000-8099)
150	
151	### Packer Plugin Downloads
152	
153	On first run, Packer will download required plugins. This may take a few minutes. If you're behind a proxy, set appropriate proxy environment variables in docker-compose.yml.
154	
155	## Benefits of Using Docker
156	
157	1. **Consistent Environment**: Same Packer, Ansible, and dependency versions across all systems
158	2. **Portability**: Works on Windows, macOS, and Linux without installing dependencies
159	3. **Isolation**: Doesn't pollute your host system with tools
160	4. **Easy Updates**: Rebuild the image to update all tools to latest versions
161	5. **Team Collaboration**: Everyone uses the same environment via the Dockerfile
162	
163	## CI/CD Integration
164	
165	### GitHub Actions Example
166	
167	```yaml
168	name: Validate Packer Templates
169	
170	on: [push, pull_request]
171	
172	jobs:
173	  validate:
174	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
175	    steps:
176	      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
177	      
178	      - name: Build Docker Image
179	        run: docker-compose build
180	      
181	      - name: Validate Templates
182	        run: docker-compose run --rm packer ./validate.sh
183	```
184	
185	### GitLab CI Example
186	
187	```yaml
188	validate:
189	  image: docker:latest
190	  services:
191	    - docker:dind
192	  script:
193	    - docker-compose build
194	    - docker-compose run --rm packer ./validate.sh
195	```
196
```
</details></tool-use>

```text
1	# Quick Setup Guide
2	
3	This guide will get you up and running with MTEA FSG Infrastructure Automation in minutes.
4	
5	## Prerequisites
6	
7	- **Docker** and **Docker Compose** installed
8	- Access to a Proxmox server (v8.0+)
9	- Proxmox API token with appropriate permissions
10	
11	## 3-Step Setup
12	
13	### 1. Clone and Setup
14	
15	```bash
16	git clone <repository-url>
17	cd <repository-name>
18	./docker-build.sh setup
19	```
20	
21	This will:
22	- Build the Docker image with all dependencies
23	- Create configuration template files in `./config/`
24	
25	### 2. Configure
26	
27	Edit the configuration files in the `config/` directory:
28	
29	**Required files to edit:**
30	
31	1. **`config/proxmox.pkrvars.hcl`** - Proxmox connection settings
32	   ```hcl
33	   proxmox_api_token_id        = "your-user@pam!your-token"
34	   proxmox_api_token_secret    = "your-secret"
35	   proxmox_hostname            = "proxmox.example.com"
36	   proxmox_node                = "your-node-name"
37	   ```
38	
39	2. **`config/build.pkrvars.hcl`** - Build user credentials
40	   ```hcl
41	   build_username           = "yourusername"
42	   build_password           = "yourpassword"
43	   build_password_encrypted = "sha512-hash"  # Generate with: mkpasswd -m sha512crypt
44	   build_key                = "your-ssh-public-key"
45	   ```
46	
47	3. **`config/ansible.pkrvars.hcl`** - Ansible user credentials
48	   ```hcl
49	   ansible_username = "ansible"
50	   ansible_key      = "ansible-ssh-public-key"
51	   ```
52	
53	4. **`config/network.pkrvars.hcl`** - Network settings
54	   ```hcl
55	   vm_bridge_interface = "vmbr0"
56	   vm_vlan_tag         = "102"
57	   # Optional: Set static IP (comment out for DHCP)
58	   # vm_ip_address = "192.168.1.100"
59	   # vm_ip_netmask = 24
60	   # vm_ip_gateway = "192.168.1.1"
61	   # vm_dns_list   = ["8.8.8.8", "8.8.4.4"]
62	   ```
63	
64	5. **`config/common.pkrvars.hcl`** - Common settings
65	   ```hcl
66	   common_iso_storage = "local"  # Your ISO storage location
67	   ```
68	
69	6. **`config/linux-storage.pkrvars.hcl`** - Storage configuration
70	   ```hcl
71	   vm_efi_storage_pool = "local-lvm"
72	   vm_storage_pool     = "local-lvm"
73	   vm_disk_device      = "vda"
74	   # Configure partitions as needed
75	   ```
76	
77	### 3. Build
78	
79	Run a build:
80	
81	```bash
82	./docker-build.sh build
83	```
84	
85	Select your desired OS from the menu.
86	
87	## Available Commands
88	
89	```bash
90	./docker-build.sh setup      # Initial setup
91	./docker-build.sh build      # Run interactive build
92	./docker-build.sh validate   # Validate all templates
93	./docker-build.sh shell      # Open shell in container
94	./docker-build.sh clean      # Remove Docker resources
95	./docker-build.sh rebuild    # Rebuild image from scratch
96	```
97	
98	## Troubleshooting
99	
100	### Config Issues
101	
102	If you get configuration errors:
103	
104	1. Ensure all required files in `config/` are edited
105	2. Check Proxmox credentials are correct
106	3. Verify network connectivity to Proxmox server
107	
108	### Build Issues
109	
110	- **Timeout errors**: Increase timeout in the OS-specific `.pkrvars.hcl` file
111	- **ISO not found**: Check `common_iso_storage` and ensure ISO is uploaded to Proxmox
112	- **Network issues**: Verify firewall allows ports 8000-8099 for HTTP server
113	
114	### Validation
115	
116	Before building, validate templates:
117	
118	```bash
119	./docker-build.sh validate
120	```
121	
122	## What Gets Built
123	
124	Templates are created in Proxmox with:
125	- Pre-installed and configured OS
126	- QEMU guest agent
127	- Cloud-init support (if enabled)
128	- SSH access configured
129	- Ansible user configured
130	- All specified packages installed
131	
132	## File Locations
133	
134	- **Configurations**: `./config/`
135	- **Build manifests**: `./manifests/`
136	- **Packer templates**: `./builds/`
137	- **Ansible roles**: `./ansible/`
138	
139	## Next Steps
140	
141	After your first successful build:
142	
143	1. Test the template by cloning it in Proxmox
144	2. Verify cloud-init configuration
145	3. Create additional builds for other OS versions
146	4. Customize storage layouts in `config/linux-storage.pkrvars.hcl`
147	5. Add custom packages in OS-specific `.pkrvars.hcl` files
148	
149	## Additional Resources
150	
151	- [DOCKER.md](DOCKER.md) - Detailed Docker usage
152	- [README.md](README.md) - Full project documentation
153	- [Proxmox API Tokens](https://pve.proxmox.com/pve-docs/pveum-plain.html) - How to create API tokens
154	
155	## Support
156	
157	For MTEA FSG team members:
158	- Internal documentation and support channels
159	- Repository issues for bugs and feature requests
160	
161	External resources:
162	- Original Project: https://github.com/ajschroeder/proxmox-packer-examples
163
```
</details></tool-use>

```md
# MTEA FSG Infrastructure Automation

**Modoc Tribal Enterprise Authority (MTEA)**  
**Federal Services Group (FSG)**  
Network Automation & Infrastructure-as-Code

---

## Table of Contents

- [Introduction](#introduction)
- [Supported Operating Systems](#supported-operating-systems)
- [Quick Start](#quick-start)
- [Requirements](#requirements)
- [Configuration Guide](#configuration-guide)
- [Building VM Templates](#building-vm-templates)
- [Docker Usage](#docker-usage)
- [Advanced Configuration](#advanced-configuration)
- [Troubleshooting](#troubleshooting)
- [CI/CD Integration](#cicd-integration)
- [Credits](#credits)

---

## Introduction

This repository provides MTEA FSG's infrastructure-as-code tooling for automating the creation of virtual machine templates on Proxmox. Built with [HashiCorp Packer](https://www.packer.io) and [Ansible](https://www.ansible.com), these tools enable consistent, repeatable deployment of standardized VM images across the MTEA FSG network infrastructure.

### Key Features

- ✅ **Automated VM Template Creation** - Build production-ready templates with a single command
- ✅ **Multi-Distribution Support** - Linux (Ubuntu, RHEL family, Debian, SUSE) and Windows 11
- ✅ **Customizable Storage Layouts** - Simple partitions, LVM, and CIS-compliant configurations
- ✅ **Network Flexibility** - Static IP or DHCP configuration
- ✅ **Cloud-Init Ready** - Modern cloud-init support for rapid provisioning
- ✅ **Dual Boot Support** - UEFI and BIOS bootloader options
- ✅ **Docker Containerized** - Consistent builds across Windows, macOS, and Linux
- ✅ **Infrastructure-as-Code** - Version controlled with HCL2

---

## Supported Operating Systems

| Operating System | Version   | Custom Storage | Static IP | UEFI | BIOS | VM ID |
| :---             | :---      | :---:          | :---:     | :---:| :---:| :---: |
| AlmaLinux        | 10, 9, 8  | ✓              | ✓         | ✓    | ✓    | random|
| CentOS Stream    | 10, 9     | ✓              | ✓         | ✓    | ✓    | 10013-14|
| Debian           | 12, 11    | ✓              | ✓         | ✓    | ✓    | 10011-12|
| OpenSUSE Leap    | 15.6, 15.5| ✓              | ✓         | ✓    | ✓    | 10009-10|
| Oracle Linux     | 9, 8      | ✓              | ✓         | ✓    | ✓    | 10007-08|
| Rocky Linux      | 10, 9, 8  | ✓              | ✓         | ✓    | ✓    | 10004-06|
| Ubuntu Server    | 26.04, 24.04, 22.04, 20.04 LTS | ✓ | ✓    | ✓    | ✓    | 10000-03, 10015|
| Windows Desktop  | 11        |                |           | ✓    |      | random|

All templates include:
- QEMU Guest Agent for improved VM management
- SSH access with key-based authentication
- Ansible automation user pre-configured
- Cloud-init support (Linux)
- Latest security updates at build time

---

## Quick Start

### Prerequisites

- [Docker](https://docs.docker.com/get-docker/) and [Docker Compose](https://docs.docker.com/compose/install/) installed
- Access to MTEA FSG Proxmox infrastructure (v8.0+)
- Proxmox API token with appropriate permissions

### 3-Step Setup

#### 1. Clone and Initialize

\```bash
git clone <repository-url>
cd <repository-name>
./docker-build.sh setup
\```

This builds the Docker image and creates configuration template files in `./config/`.

#### 2. Configure for MTEA FSG

Edit configuration files in the `config/` directory:

**Essential configuration files:**

- **`proxmox.pkrvars.hcl`** - Proxmox API credentials and node
- **`build.pkrvars.hcl`** - VM build user credentials  
- **`ansible.pkrvars.hcl`** - Ansible automation user
- **`network.pkrvars.hcl`** - MTEA FSG network (VLANs, subnets)
- **`linux-storage.pkrvars.hcl`** - Storage pools and partitions

<details>
<summary>Example: Proxmox Configuration</summary>

\```hcl
// config/proxmox.pkrvars.hcl
proxmox_api_token_id        = "packer@pam!packer-token"
proxmox_api_token_secret    = "<your-api-token-secret>"
proxmox_insecure_connection = true
proxmox_hostname            = "proxmox.mtea.local"
proxmox_node                = "pve-node-01"
\```
</details>

<details>
<summary>Example: Network Configuration</summary>

\```hcl
// config/network.pkrvars.hcl
vm_bridge_interface = "vmbr0"
vm_vlan_tag         = "100"

// Optional: Static IP (defaults to DHCP if commented)
// vm_ip_address = "10.10.10.100"
// vm_ip_netmask = 24
// vm_ip_gateway = "10.10.10.1"
// vm_dns_list   = ["10.10.10.10", "10.10.10.11"]
\```
</details>

#### 3. Build a Template

\```bash
./docker-build.sh build
\```

Select your desired OS from the interactive menu (e.g., option 15 for Ubuntu 24.04 LTS).

### Validation

Validate all templates before building:

\```bash
./docker-build.sh validate
\```

---

## Requirements

### Infrastructure Platform
- **Proxmox VE** 8.0 or later

### Docker Environment (Recommended)
- Docker 20.10+
- Docker Compose 2.0+

All dependencies (Packer, Ansible, plugins) are included in the Docker image.

### Manual Installation (Alternative)

<details>
<summary>Ubuntu - Install Packer & Ansible</summary>

\```bash
# Add HashiCorp repository
sudo bash -c 'wget -O- https://apt.releases.hashicorp.com/gpg | gpg --dearmor > /usr/share/keyrings/hashicorp-archive-keyring.gpg'
sudo bash -c 'echo "deb [signed-by=/usr/share/keyrings/hashicorp-archive-keyring.gpg] \
https://apt.releases.hashicorp.com $(lsb_release -cs) main" > /etc/apt/sources.list.d/hashicorp.list'

# Install Packer
sudo apt update && sudo apt install packer

# Install Ansible
sudo apt install -y software-properties-common
sudo add-apt-repository --yes --update ppa:ansible/ansible
sudo apt install -y ansible-core
\```
</details>

<details>
<summary>CentOS/RHEL - Install Packer & Ansible</summary>

\```bash
# Install Packer
sudo yum install -y yum-utils
sudo yum-config-manager --add-repo https://rpm.releases.hashicorp.com/RHEL/hashicorp.repo
sudo yum -y install packer

# Install Ansible
sudo dnf -y install ansible
\```
</details>

**Required Packer Plugins** (auto-downloaded on first run):
- [Packer Plugin for Git](https://github.com/ethanmdavidson/packer-plugin-git) 0.6.2+
- [Packer Plugin for Proxmox](https://developer.hashicorp.com/packer/integrations/hashicorp/proxmox) 1.2.1 (pinned)

---

## Configuration Guide

### Directory Structure

\```
mtea-fsg-automation/
├── ansible/          # Ansible roles for OS configuration
├── builds/           # Packer templates per OS
├── config/           # Your MTEA FSG-specific settings (YOU EDIT THESE)
├── manifests/        # Build manifests (auto-generated)
└── tests/            # Validation test suites
\```

### Configuration Scripts

Generate configuration templates:

\```bash
./config.sh              # Creates ./config/ directory
./config.sh dev          # Creates ./dev/ directory (for multiple environments)
./config.sh prod         # Creates ./prod/ directory
\```

Then customize the `.pkrvars.hcl` files for your MTEA FSG infrastructure.

### Essential Configuration Files

#### 1. Proxmox Connection (`config/proxmox.pkrvars.hcl`)

\```hcl
proxmox_api_token_id        = "packer@pam!packer-token"
proxmox_api_token_secret    = "<api-token-secret>"
proxmox_insecure_connection = true  // false with valid TLS cert
proxmox_hostname            = "proxmox.mtea.local"
proxmox_node                = "pve-node-01"
\```

**API Token Setup**: Token must have `PVEAdmin` role. See [Proxmox API documentation](https://pve.proxmox.com/pve-docs/pveum-plain.html).

#### 2. Build User Credentials (`config/build.pkrvars.hcl`)

\```hcl
build_username           = "mtea-admin"
build_password           = "<plaintext-password>"
build_password_encrypted = "<sha512-hash>"
build_key                = "<ssh-public-key>"
\```

Generate SHA-512 password hash:
\```bash
mkpasswd -m sha512crypt
\```

Generate SSH key:
\```bash
ssh-keygen -t ecdsa -b 521 -C "mtea-fsg-automation"
\```

#### 3. Ansible User (`config/ansible.pkrvars.hcl`)

\```hcl
ansible_username = "ansible"
ansible_key      = "<ansible-ssh-public-key>"
\```

#### 4. Network Settings (`config/network.pkrvars.hcl`)

\```hcl
// Proxmox Network
vm_bridge_interface = "vmbr0"
vm_vlan_tag         = "100"

// Optional: Static IP (comment out for DHCP)
// vm_ip_address = "10.10.10.100"
// vm_ip_netmask = 24
// vm_ip_gateway = "10.10.10.1"
// vm_dns_list   = ["10.10.10.10", "10.10.10.11"]
\```

#### 5. Storage Configuration (`config/linux-storage.pkrvars.hcl`)

\```hcl
vm_storage_pool     = "local-lvm"
vm_efi_storage_pool = "local-lvm"
vm_disk_device      = "vda"
vm_disk_use_swap    = true
\```

<details>
<summary>Storage Layout: Simple Single Partition (UEFI)</summary>

\```hcl
vm_disk_partitions = [
  {
    name = "efi", size = 1024,
    format = { label = "EFIFS", fstype = "fat32" },
    mount = { path = "/boot/efi", options = "" },
    volume_group = "",
  },
  {
    name = "boot", size = 1024,
    format = { label = "BOOTFS", fstype = "ext4" },
    mount = { path = "/boot", options = "" },
    volume_group = "",
  },
  {
    name = "root", size = -1,  // All remaining space
    format = { label = "ROOTFS", fstype = "ext4" },
    mount = { path = "/", options = "" },
    volume_group = "",
  },
]
\```
</details>

<details>
<summary>Storage Layout: LVM with CIS-Compliant Hardening</summary>

\```hcl
vm_disk_partitions = [
  { name = "efi", size = 1024, format = { label = "EFIFS", fstype = "fat32" },
    mount = { path = "/boot/efi", options = "" }, volume_group = "" },
  { name = "boot", size = 1024, format = { label = "BOOTFS", fstype = "ext4" },
    mount = { path = "/boot", options = "" }, volume_group = "" },
  { name = "sysvg", size = -1, format = { label = "", fstype = "" },
    mount = { path = "", options = "" }, volume_group = "sysvg" },
]

vm_disk_lvm = [
  {
    name: "sysvg",
    partitions: [
      { name = "lv_root", size = 10240, format = { label = "ROOTFS", fstype = "ext4" },
        mount = { path = "/", options = "" }},
      { name = "lv_home", size = 4096, format = { label = "HOMEFS", fstype = "ext4" },
        mount = { path = "/home", options = "nodev,nosuid" }},
      { name = "lv_tmp", size = 4096, format = { label = "TMPFS", fstype = "ext4" },
        mount = { path = "/tmp", options = "nodev,noexec,nosuid" }},
      { name = "lv_var", size = 2048, format = { label = "VARFS", fstype = "ext4" },
        mount = { path = "/var", options = "nodev" }},
      { name = "lv_var_log", size = 4096, format = { label = "VARLOGFS", fstype = "ext4" },
        mount = { path = "/var/log", options = "nodev,noexec,nosuid" }},
    ],
  }
]
\```
</details>

#### 6. Common Settings (`config/common.pkrvars.hcl`)

\```hcl
common_iso_storage      = "local"  // Proxmox storage with ISO files
common_data_source      = "http"   // or "disk" for isolated networks
common_http_port_min    = 8000
common_http_port_max    = 8099
common_ip_wait_timeout  = "20m"
common_shutdown_timeout = "15m"
\```

**Firewall Configuration** (if using `http` data source):

\```bash
# iptables
iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --match multiport --dports 8000:8099 -j ACCEPT

# firewalld
firewall-cmd --zone=public --add-port=8000-8099/tcp --permanent
firewall-cmd --reload
\```

---

## Building VM Templates

### Interactive Build

\```bash
./build.sh
# Or with Docker:
./docker-build.sh build
\```

Select from the menu:
- Options 1-14: Various Linux distributions
- Option 15: Ubuntu 24.04 LTS
- Option 21: Ubuntu 26.04 LTS
- Options 18-20: Windows 11

### Debug Mode

\```bash
./build.sh --debug
# Or with Docker:
docker-compose run --rm packer ./build.sh --debug
\```

Enables verbose output and pauses on errors.

### Build Process

1. **Initialize**: Packer downloads required plugins (first run)
2. **Create VM**: Temporary VM created in Proxmox
3. **Boot & Install**: Automated OS installation from ISO
4. **Provision**: Ansible applies configuration, updates, packages
5. **Convert**: VM converted to reusable template
6. **Manifest**: Build metadata saved to `manifests/`

### Template Features

All templates include:
- QEMU Guest Agent
- SSH access (key-based)
- Ansible automation user
- Cloud-init support (Linux)
- Latest security patches
- Customizable storage layouts

### Multiple Environments

\```bash
# Development environment
./config.sh dev
./build.sh dev

# Production environment
./config.sh prod
./build.sh prod
\```

---

## Docker Usage

### Quick Commands

\```bash
./docker-build.sh setup      # Initial setup (first time)
./docker-build.sh build      # Run interactive build
./docker-build.sh validate   # Validate all templates
./docker-build.sh shell      # Open shell in container
./docker-build.sh clean      # Remove Docker resources
./docker-build.sh rebuild    # Rebuild image from scratch
\```

### Using Docker Compose Directly

\```bash
# Build image
docker-compose build

# Run interactively
docker-compose run --rm packer

# Run specific command
docker-compose run --rm packer ./validate.sh

# Debug mode
docker-compose run --rm packer ./build.sh --debug
\```

### Custom Config Path

\```bash
docker-compose run --rm packer ./build.sh /workspace/my-custom-config
\```

### Environment Variables

Enable Packer logging:

\```bash
PACKER_LOG=1 docker-compose run --rm packer ./build.sh
\```

Or add to `docker-compose.yml`:

\```yaml
environment:
  - PACKER_LOG=1
  - PACKER_LOG_PATH=/workspace/packer.log
\```

### Volume Mounts

By default, the following are mounted:
- `./` → `/workspace` (entire project)
- `./config` → `/workspace/config` (your configurations)
- `./manifests` → `/workspace/manifests` (build outputs)
- `~/.ssh` → `/root/.ssh:ro` (SSH keys, read-only)

### Network Mode

Uses `host` network mode for Proxmox connectivity and HTTP server. Modify `network_mode` in `docker-compose.yml` if needed.

### Building Without Docker Compose

\```bash
# Build image
docker build -t mtea-fsg-automation:latest .

# Run interactively
docker run -it --rm --network host \
  -v "$(pwd):/workspace" \
  -v "$(pwd)/config:/workspace/config" \
  -v "$(pwd)/manifests:/workspace/manifests" \
  -v "$HOME/.ssh:/root/.ssh:ro" \
  mtea-fsg-automation:latest

# Run single command
docker run -it --rm --network host \
  -v "$(pwd):/workspace" \
  -v "$(pwd)/config:/workspace/config" \
  mtea-fsg-automation:latest \
  ./validate.sh
\```

### Docker Benefits

- ✅ **Consistent Environment** - Same tools/versions across all systems
- ✅ **Portability** - Works on Windows, macOS, Linux without manual setup
- ✅ **Isolation** - No pollution of host system
- ✅ **Easy Updates** - Rebuild image to update tools
- ✅ **Team Collaboration** - Everyone uses identical environment

---

## Advanced Configuration

### Per-OS Template Settings

Each OS has a config file: `config/linux-<os>-<version>.pkrvars.hcl`

Example `config/linux-ubuntu-24-04-lts.pkrvars.hcl`:

\```hcl
// Guest OS Settings
vm_os_language = "en_US"
vm_os_keyboard = "us"
vm_os_timezone = "America/Los_Angeles"

// Hardware
vm_cpu_count   = 2
vm_cpu_sockets = 1
vm_cpu_type    = "host"  // "host" for best performance
vm_mem_size    = 4096
vm_disk_size   = "40G"

// Boot & Storage
vm_bios        = "ovmf"  // UEFI (or "seabios" for BIOS)
vm_disk_type   = "virtio"
vm_disk_format = "raw"

// Cloud-Init
vm_cloudinit   = true

// ISO
iso_path       = "iso"
iso_file       = "ubuntu-24.04-live-server-amd64.iso"
iso_checksum   = "https://releases.ubuntu.com/noble/SHA256SUMS"
\```

### Cloud-Init Support

Enable/disable per OS:

\```hcl
vm_cloudinit           = true   // Enable cloud-init
vm_cloudinit_disk_type = "ide"  // or "scsi", "virtio"
\```

When cloning templates with cloud-init:
- Set hostname and network
- Inject SSH keys
- Run custom scripts
- Configure users and packages

### Terraform Integration

Deploy VMs from templates using Terraform. See:
- `example_uefi_ubuntu_terraform/` - Ubuntu deployment example
- `terraform_import/` - Import existing VMs

---

## Troubleshooting

### Configuration Issues

**Problem**: Build fails with config errors

**Solution**:
1. Verify all required config files edited
2. Check Proxmox credentials are correct
3. Test connectivity: `ping proxmox.mtea.local`

### Build Timeouts

**Problem**: Build exceeds timeout

**Solution**: Increase timeout in OS-specific variables:

\```hcl
// In config/linux-ubuntu-24-04-lts.pkrvars.hcl
variable "timeout" {
  default = "120m"  // Increase from default 90m
}
\```

### ISO Not Found

**Problem**: Packer can't find ISO file

**Solution**:
1. Verify ISO uploaded to Proxmox storage
2. Check `common_iso_storage` matches storage name
3. Confirm `iso_path` and `iso_file` are correct
4. Test: Navigate to Proxmox web UI → Storage → ISO images

### Network Connectivity

**Problem**: Can't reach Proxmox or HTTP server timeouts

**Solution**:
1. Verify `vm_bridge_interface` exists: `pvesh get /nodes/<node>/network`
2. Check VLAN tag matches network config
3. Ensure firewall allows ports 8000-8099
4. Test: `curl http://<packer-host>:8000/`
5. For Docker: Verify `network_mode: host` in docker-compose.yml

### SSH Connection Failures

**Problem**: Packer can't SSH to VM

**Solution**:
1. Verify credentials in `config/build.pkrvars.hcl`
2. Check VM has network connectivity (console in Proxmox)
3. Verify QEMU guest agent starting: `systemctl status qemu-guest-agent`
4. Check Proxmox firewall rules

### Packer Plugin Errors

**Problem**: Plugin download or compatibility issues

**Solution**: Re-initialize plugins:

\```bash
cd builds/linux/ubuntu/24-04-lts/
packer init .
\```

### Permission Issues (Docker)

**Problem**: File permission errors on mounted volumes

**Solution**:

\```bash
sudo chown -R $(id -u):$(id -g) config/ manifests/
\```

### Proxmox Connection Issues (Docker)

**Checklist**:
- [ ] Container can reach Proxmox (check `proxmox_hostname`)
- [ ] Network mode is `host` in docker-compose.yml
- [ ] Firewall allows HTTP ports 8000-8099
- [ ] No proxy blocking connections

---

## CI/CD Integration

### GitHub Actions

\```yaml
name: Validate Packer Templates

on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  validate:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Build Docker Image
        run: docker-compose build
      
      - name: Validate Templates
        run: docker-compose run --rm packer ./validate.sh
\```

### GitLab CI

\```yaml
validate:
  image: docker:latest
  services:
    - docker:dind
  script:
    - docker-compose build
    - docker-compose run --rm packer ./validate.sh
\```

---

## Known Issues

### Windows Builds

Windows templates require:
- Valid product keys (not included)
- Correct OS image names in config
- VirtIO drivers for storage/network

See `config/windows-desktop-11.pkrvars.hcl.example` for configuration details.

### Proxmox API Permissions

Currently requires `PVEAdmin` role. Least-privilege configuration documentation in progress.

### Packer Proxmox Plugin

Pinned to v1.2.1 due to [CPU bug](https://github.com/hashicorp/packer-plugin-proxmox/issues/307) in newer versions.
